questions.question: Explain the message-passing programming model in distributed memory systems. What are the fundamental send and receive operations, and how does blocking differ from non-blocking communication?
keyPoints: Sender specifies destination process rank and message buffer
Receiver specifies source rank and receiving buffer
Blocking send (MPI_Ssend) waits for matching receive - ensures synchronization
Non-blocking send (MPI_Isend) returns immediately - allows overlap of communication and computation
Deadlock can occur with blocking operations if send/receive ordering is incorrect

questions.question: Derive the communication cost formula T = α + βm for point-to-point message passing. Explain the physical significance of α (latency) and β (inverse bandwidth) parameters.
keyPoints: α represents fixed startup cost: software overhead, protocol handling, router traversal
β represents per-byte transmission cost determined by physical bandwidth
Total time = startup overhead + transmission time = α + β × m
For small messages (m < α/β), latency dominates; for large messages, bandwidth dominates
These parameters are measured experimentally using ping-pong benchmarks

questions.question: Compare the hypercube and 2D mesh interconnection topologies in terms of diameter, bisection bandwidth, and degree. Which topology scales better and why?
keyPoints: Hypercube diameter = log₂N; 2D mesh diameter ≈ 2√N
Hypercube bisection bandwidth = N/2; 2D mesh = √N × √N/2 = N/2
Hypercube degree = log₂N (scales with N); 2D mesh degree = 4 (constant)
Hypercube offers better connectivity but requires more links per node
For scalability, constant-degree networks (mesh, torus) are preferred for large systems despite larger diameter

questions.question: What is the isoefficiency function in parallel algorithm analysis? Derive why a parallel algorithm with communication cost O(P) has better scalability than one with cost O(P²).
keyPoints: Isoefficiency function relates problem size W to processors P needed to maintain constant efficiency
For T_parallel = W/P + T_comm, if T_comm = O(P), then W must grow as O(P) to keep efficiency constant
If T_comm = O(P²), W must grow as O(P²) to maintain efficiency - much worse scalability
Lower communication complexity enables better utilization of additional processors
Strong scalability: fixed problem size; weak scalability: problem grows with processor count

questions.question: Explain the halo exchange pattern used in stencil computations on distributed memory systems. Why is this pattern necessary and how does it relate to ghost cells?
keyPoints: Stencil computations require values from neighboring grid points
Each processor owns a subdomain and exchanges boundary values with neighbors before computation
Ghost cells (padding) store received boundary values locally
Halo exchange involves send/receive pairs in each dimension for all boundaries
Pattern enables local computation without remote memory access during the stencil operation

questions.question: Design an MPI program for parallel matrix-vector multiplication y = Ax where matrix A is distributed by rows across P processors. Explain the data distribution, local computation, and communication pattern required. Derive the time complexity for this algorithm.
keyPoints: Distribute rows of A evenly across processors - each processor gets n/P rows
Vector x must be broadcast to all processors before local computation
Each processor computes partial y_i = Σ A_ij × x_j for its local rows
After local computation, reduction operation combines partial results at root processor
Time complexity: T = O(n²/P) for computation + O(n) for broadcast + O(n) for reduction
Total: O(n²/P + n), demonstrating parallel speedup of approximately P for large n

questions.question: Consider a 3D torus network with dimensions k × k × k. Derive expressions for: (a) total number of nodes, (b) network diameter, (c) bisection bandwidth, and (d) degree of each node. Compare these metrics with a hypercube having the same number of nodes.
keyPoints: (a) Total nodes = k³
(b) Diameter = 3⌈k/2⌉ (worst case: opposite corners in each dimension with wraparound)
(c) Bisection bandwidth = k² (minimum cut divides one dimension, revealing k² links)
(d) Degree = 6 (each node connects to ±x, ±y, ±z directions)
For k³ = 2^d hypercube: d = 3log₂k, diameter = 3log₂k, degree = d, bisection = k³/2
Torus has constant degree (6) vs hypercube degree growing with log(k), making torus more scalable for large systems

questions.question: Analyze the following deadlock scenario in an MPI program using blocking sends: Process 0 calls MPI_Ssend to Process 1 while Process 1 calls MPI_Ssend to Process 0 simultaneously. Explain why this causes deadlock and propose two different solutions to avoid it.
keyPoints: Both processes block on synchronous send waiting for matching receives
Neither process has posted its receive, so both senders block indefinitely
Solution 1: Post receives before sends (receive-first approach)
Solution 2: Use non-blocking sends (MPI_Isend) and waits (MPI_Wait)
Solution 3: Use MPI_Sendrecv which atomically performs both operations
Solution 4: Ensure communication ordering follows a consistent pattern across all processes
